14.2.label: Question 14.2
14.2.question:
List all the factors that might cause the future morbidity of new policyholders to differ from that
of your own historical experience data, for a particular health insurance product.

Factors that might cause the future morb:          changes in the target market (this will happen to some extent unintentionally every time
the product is altered, since different products will appeal to different people; it might
also change substantially for economic / fiscal / cultural reasons)
         changes in the distribution channel and the territory targeted
         changes in the impact of medical science, such as rate of diagnosis of critical illness, health
care provision, treatments / cures, health education, nutrition
         changes in State provision of healthcare (particularly affecting PMI experience)
         changes in selective withdrawals – if the insurer’s group of policyholders experiences a
different level of withdrawals by policyholders of below‐average health from that
experienced by the group of lives underlying the investigation
\uf0b7 changes in policy wording:          changes to the economy – eg IP and PMI claim experience tends to be worse during

14.3.label: Question 14.3
14.3.scenario: State how you expect recovery rates to vary in relation to the following factors:

(i).question: the length of time since the policyholder fell sick

Duration since the policyholder fell sic: The majority of sickness durations are very short, usually under 6 months in length. Individuals
who are still sick after 6 months will often remain sick for very long periods of time.
Therefore recovery rates typically decrease with increasing duration since the start of sickness,
reflecting the reducing proportion of the less serious cases remaining (ie the increasing proportion

(ii).question: the length of the deferred period of the policy

Deferred period: The longer the deferred period, the more likely the policyholder is to recover before the claim
begins. Therefore for very short deferred periods, recovery rates may be significantly higher than
The length of the deferred period can also affect the type of individual taking out a policy,
affecting recovery rates further. For example, individuals who believe they are only likely to make
claims for longer sickness durations may choose longer deferred periods. (If they then do tend to
suffer from long‐duration sicknesses, recovery rates will be lower.)

(iii).question: the length of time since claim payments were started.

Duration since the start of claim paymen: Similarly to part (i), recovery rates typically decrease with increasing duration since the start of
claim. Therefore recovery rates tend to be higher in the period immediately after claim payments
This effect will be more pronounced the shorter the deferred period is (as explained above).
Note that the underlying variable affecting recovery rates is the time since the onset of sickness
So, provided all other factors (like the type of lives insured) are the same, then the deferred period
should not affect the rate of recovery as at a particular duration since the start of the illness,
however it will affect the rate of recovery from the start of the claim.
However, all other factors will not typically be the same. For example, the amount of claims
control is likely to be highest early on in a claim. Consider two claimants, both of whom have been
sick for 15 months. One has a deferred period of a year and so has only been claiming benefits for
3 months while the other has a deferred period of 3 months, so has been claiming benefits for a
year. It is likely that the recovery rate for the policyholder who has only been claiming for 3
months is higher than for the policyholder who has already been claiming for a year as a result of
having recently benefited from rehabilitation assistance, such as counselling, (whereas the longer‐
term claimant is less likely to have received such assistance recently).
Note also that there might be recovery “spikes”, ie a sudden increase in recoveries at certain
points in time, eg a year after sickness / claim inception. This would be more pronounced if there
is some kind of change at that point in time, eg a fall in benefits or further claims management

14.4.label: Question 14.4
14.4.scenario:
A health and care insurer has been selling a wide range of health insurance products, including IP

insurance, for many years. It is preparing to launch a short‐term accident and sickness insurance
policy under which benefits will only be payable for up to two years following claim inception.
The insurer is considering a number of markets in which the product is to be sold.
14.4.question:
Describe how the insurer would determine the assumptions for claim inception and termination
rates to be used in the pricing basis for the sickness benefit. You do not need to detail how
experience investigations would be carried out.

Solution: The insurer will have no directly relevant data, but it will have experience from its own IP
business.                                                                                            [½]
A suitable investigation period should be chosen, ...                                                [½]
... and both claims and exposed to risk data should be split into appropriate groups ...             [½]
... eg by group and individual, deferred period etc.                                                 [½]
The analysis would compare actual inception and termination rates against those expected
according to some standard basis.                                                                    [½]
Termination rates occurring at claim durations of over two years can be ignored.                     [½]
These rates will need to be adjusted to:          the expected experience of the new target market and distribution channels                 [½]
         changes in underwriting standards and claims control                                       [½]
         different product design features, eg definition of sickness                                [½]
         credibility of data, eg the new product may offer deferred periods for which there are few
or no data on the existing IP product                                                   [½]
         trends from past data, extrapolated into the future                                         [½]
         anomalies in past data or future expected experience due to unusual economic
         other anticipated future changes, such as medical advances.                                [½]
The insurer may need to use other sources of data, if available, to assess what adjustments to
For example:          reinsurer’s data on short‐term accident experience                                         [½]
         industry data, to supplement its own data where experience is limited (eg existing standard
If average premium rates are to be calculated for particular groups or lenders, then average claim
inception and termination rates might be used.                                                  [½]
These will be calculated based on the mix of business expected (eg in terms of age, gender etc) for
The insurer might compare the resultant premium rates with those of its competitors, to check on
overall suitability of the inception and termination rate assumptions.                        [½]

14.5.label: Question 14.5
14.5.scenario:
State how you would expect a deterioration of the economic situation to affect withdrawal rates
for the following regular premium contracts:

Why this might be a problem: Large policies are subsidising small policies. The degree of overpayment by large policies could be
so great as to make them very poor value for money, going against principles of equity and quite

Solution: The risk here is in selling more smaller‐sized policies than anticipated (either more in absolute
terms, or as a proportion of the new business portfolio). If this happens the company might not
recoup the variable expenses. In addition, the contribution to fixed expenses would be smaller
than necessary.
The future volume is a function of competition, which might itself be a function of price. The
price is a function of the expense loading, which is itself a function of future volume. So future
volume is a function of future volume.
In order to get round this, the insurer could assume some bands of price, say, across which future
volume is insensitive, so as to home in on a final answer.
The ‘non‐medical limit’ is the maximum benefit for which one can apply for cover without
automatically requiring specified underwriting evidence (usually a medical examination or a
private medical attendant’s report).
Hence, every policy application for benefits above this limit will require this further evidence.
However, applications for lower benefit amounts may still require further evidence if the
underwriters feel that this is necessary (eg the information supplied on the application form
suggests that the applicant is a high risk).
This is the risk that the intermediary will default on its payment of commission clawback, for
example, as a result of becoming bankrupt between the time of sale and the time of policy
withdrawal.
The greater the likelihood of this event, the less credit the insurer should allow in its pricing basis
for clawback of commission.
The actuary must make assumptions as to early lapse rates and estimate the proportions of
the unearned commission that will be reclaimed.
Why this is not a problem for PMI: It is not a problem for PMI because premiums are not expressed as a rate per sum insured or
benefit amount, so the question of cross‐subsidy (at least between large and small policies) does
not arise. Each policy will pay whatever per‐policy expense loading was assumed in its pricing
Here are some possible ways of correcting for this problem, where it occurs.
An area of risk relates to the inclusion of expenses that do not vary by size of contract into
the charging structure. The following are examples of ways to handle this problem:
\uf0b7 individual calculation of: With this method, the actuary simply calculates the premium for the contract using the
actual benefit level, age, term etc for the policy in question, loading explicitly for the
per‐policy expenses. This would probably only be done for the large cases: very small
policies priced on this basis would end up paying a very large percentage of their premium
as an expense loading – the premium rate would then seem unreasonably large.
         policy fee addition to the premium – or deduction from income benefits
This approach may need to be modified if the resultant policy fee or charges would
make premiums uncompetitive.
This is the same problem as was mentioned for smaller policies using the first method.
The idea is that the policy fee would represent the per‐policy expense costs, and the
premium rate would only cover the other, proportionate, expenses. However, the policy
fee may need to be reduced from its theoretical level in order to make the total premiums
for small policies more competitive (and, perhaps more importantly, more reasonable).
But to avoid not covering expenses, the reduction will need to be compensated for
elsewhere.
So the actuary will reduce the policy fee to the level required on grounds of
reasonableness and/or competitiveness, given where the insurer wants to be in the
market. The contribution to expenses determined as a percentage of premium will then
need to be increased. A model portfolio would be used to ensure that the total expense
contribution received by the insurer, for the assumed volume and mix of business, would
be the same as what it would have been using the theoretically correct premium rates
and policy fee.
However, any such modification would lead to increased risk for the company.
Question: Initial commission paid at the start of a policy has a two‐year earnings period, such that a
clawback of 50% of the initial commission (without interest) is made on a lapse at the end of the
first year. Lapse can only occur at the end of each policy year.
Calculate the expected amount of clawback payable at the end of the first year, assuming lapse
rates of 10% and that 5% of clawback payments are not made. Express your answer as a
percentage of the initial commission paid per policy. Ignore mortality.
Different premium rates are charged according to the band into which the requested
benefit falls. This would require an estimation of the number of policies likely to
Provided the premium rates charged for each band are the theoretically correct ones for
the average benefit level for each band, then the insurer really only needs to know the
expected numbers of policies in each band for practical purposes. For example, fixing a
band level to be higher than any benefit size reasonably found in practice would be a
Of the above three ways of dealing with expenses that are invariant by size of contract, the most
common approach is the second method (policy fee addition). However, the approaches are not
mutually exclusive: when the third approach is used it is also often combined with some form of
Fixed expenses: Fixed expenses, such as the costs of developing a new product, also need to be recouped.
Expense loadings need to allow for the likely volumes of business sold, and this is a
particular area of uncertainty for a competitive product line.
The amount of loading for these purposes should be such that, given the anticipated volume of
sales of the product over its expected lifetime, the total loadings received will at least recoup the
direct expenses of the product (both fixed and variable) and pay a fair share towards the insurer’s
overheads. Provided the new business assumptions do not turn out to have been too optimistic,
the product should make an additional contribution to profit. The new business assumptions
must themselves reflect the expected competitive position of the product in the market over the
3.5 Comparison with life insurance: Generally, more work is required to put a health and care insurance product on the books
than for a life insurance only product. The main reason for this is the extra underwriting
effort required – non-medical limits are often lower; more information may be required and
more time is taken to assess the morbidity.
This term is also defined in the Glossar: Product development costs might also be higher as more effort is needed to derive
incidence rates, develop policy literature and train sales staff. Hence initial expenses are
Furthermore, unlike life insurance where the consumer often sees a need for the product,
healthcare insurance products normally have to be sold, which can involve considerable
time in explaining the key features of the product and why it is beneficial for the client to
One of the reasons why life insurance is more ‘popular’ is because it often needs to be purchased
as a condition of taking out a loan to buy a house.
Claims expenses are also more onerous than for a life insurance product. More time and
medical information will be required to determine the validity of a claim, ie whether it meets
More time and expense will also be involved in the maintenance of a claim for policies where an
\uf0b7 monitoring claim validity:          enforcing changes to benefit entitlement (eg reducing benefits under IP during recovery
when some partial employment becomes possible for the claimant)
         efforts expended on rehabilitation (eg helping IP claimants to return to work)
4 Expense inflation: Any product that offers a level premium in return for an increasing risk must incorporate an
element of expense inflation, even if the premiums are annually reviewable.
This means that, when setting the premium rate that will apply over the period of cover,
assumptions about the inflation increases throughout the policy term will need to be made. (This
is similar to the discussion in Section 2 about benefit inflation.)
This comment also applies where premiums are increasing, eg at a fixed rate or in line with an
index. In the latter case, the expense inflation assumption should be consistent with that
assumed for the index.
A reviewable contract guarantees that cover will be provided for more than one year (the actual
period being specified), but is subject to periodic reviews of the premiums that apply. However,
premiums would only be changed (at a review date) if there is a change in the expectation, by the
insurer, of the future experience of the portfolio of business to which the policy belongs. The
change in expectation will typically be as a result of overall experience being different from that
assumed in the pricing basis.
With no change in expectation between review dates, the premiums would not be changed.
Since inflation of future expenses is expected, allowance for this will be included in the premium
being charged at any stage. So the fact that premiums are reviewable does not alter the need to
allow for expense inflation when calculating the premium.
Notice the difference here between a policy being reviewed and being renewed. If a policy is
renewable annually (like PMI), then the period of cover is only one year, after which premiums
can be increased to reflect increases in expenses etc. In practice, for short‐term policies, an
implicit allowance for expense inflation would be made by simply increasing per‐policy expenses
to the level that would apply at the average time those expenses are expected to be incurred.
An inflation assumption will be necessary to apply to loadings in respect of renewal
administration, claim administration and policy termination expenses.
Rates of inflation will be partly related to prices and partly related to salary costs.
Expenses would be expected to be mostly related to salary costs because the majority of an
insurance company’s costs are staff costs.
4.1 Data and adjustments: The recent expense inflation experience of the company (or, if not available, of the industry)
should be analysed to determine the basis for future projection.
This is to determine the way in which the insurer’s expense inflation behaves. For example, if past
expense inflation has appeared to reflect 20% price inflation plus 80% earnings inflation, then an
appropriate future inflation assumption would be the average of the expected future price and
earnings inflation rates, weighted in the same proportions (provided there are no reasons for the
weightings to be different in future).
\uf0b7 expected future rates of:          the differential between the return on government fixed interest securities and on
government index-linked securities, where such exist
         the differential between the return on fixed interest government bonds and on
\uf0b7 recent actual experience: The third approach is really just one way of arriving at the expected future inflation of the second
point. Also, it is only approximate – it will be out by the extent of any inflation risk premium
implicit in the price of the government fixed interest bonds.
When pricing a contract, it is important:          the inflation of expenses during the term of a future new policy, from the issue date to its
         the inflation of all expenses between ‘now’ (the date at which premium rates are being
set) and the dates at which the future new policies are actually issued.
As an example of the second point, the insurer might set its per‐policy initial expense assumption
at £250, but for a policy issued in one year’s time it perhaps ought to be £260, for one issued in
two years’ time it should be £272, and so on.
So the insurer needs to make the overall future expense assumptions appropriate to the future
period over which it would expect the pricing basis to be used. In the case above, for example, if it
expected its basis to apply for the next four years, it might fix its initial expense assumption at
Commission: Commission is often paid at a higher rate on the initial premium than on subsequent
premiums, to reflect the additional work involved in the process of sale.
This may be influenced by current levels of sales remuneration in the market place and/or
by any legislatively imposed rates of commission.
Commission structures were discussed in: These assumptions will be determined by the amounts that the insurer intends to pay the
Clawback: Where the salesperson is paid a higher commission in year one than in subsequent years,
there is a probability that the insurer will lose out if the policyholder lapses at an early
duration and the product has a level annual commission loading in the premium.
This will essentially be the case whenever a policyholder pays a regular premium and the
salesperson gets a higher commission at the start of the policy than at any later time. So this
would also include policies of one‐year terms payable by monthly premiums, which have single
commission payments made at the start of the contract.
Under these circumstances, the commission is not deemed to be ‘earned’ for a number of
months into the policy term, although it is paid to the salesperson. The salesperson may be
required to repay part of the initial commission if the policyholder lapses the policy (other
than through a claim) during this earning period (as described in Chapter 8).
The insurer thus takes a ‘credit risk’ on the initial commission paid, and the commission
loading should not assume full clawback will be obtained in all cases where it is
theoretically due.
Experience by distribution type will be the best guide to the relevant numbers. The
standard approach compares the numbers of early lapses against the policies ‘exposed’ at
each duration subject to clawback. The analysis would be split by product type also –
though credibility of numbers may be an issue here.
In-house parameters may be compared to any market figures available and those provided
by reinsurers or consultants. Again, this will be monitored and amended in the light of
emerging experience.
5.2 Adjustments: The pricing actuary will need to include any special arrangements that have been agreed
In cases where a single tariff of health insurance premiums is to be sold by different
distributors, each receiving different commission structures, the pricing actuary will need to
make forecasts of the volumes of business to be sold under each arrangement.
These assumptions on volumes have to be monitored against actual experience and
So, an important additional assumption is the mix of future new business by distribution channel.
5.3 Loading commission into premiums: This enables the appropriate rates to be loaded into the premium formula, on average, and
thus the overall commission loadings will support the commission paid.
Commission can be expressed as a percentage of premium, or as a percentage of the sum
insured, or occasionally as a fixed sum.
This is very similar to the discussion of loading other expenses (above).
5.4 Comparison with life insurance: Levels of remuneration may be higher than on standard life insurance. A higher level of
initial commission could be justified on a health and care insurance policy due to the extra
effort needed on the part of the salesperson. This involves both explaining the benefits to a
prospective policyholder and collecting the extra information required for the underwriting
6 Investment return: Any assumption for investment return should be consistent with the basis chosen
Consistency is an important principle when setting assumptions. Therefore the implied
relationship between the investment and inflation assumptions should be realistic.
The value assigned to this parameter wil:          the significance of the assumption for the profitability of the contract, which will
depend on the level of reserves built up and the duration of the policy
         the intended investment mix for the contract, as affected by reinvestment (see
below), the current return on the investments within that mix and, where appropriate,
         the extent of any reinvestment risk and the extent to which this can be reduced by a
suitable choice of assets – the less important the reinvestment risk, the less account
needs to be taken of future investment yields.
The significance of the assumption: The first step in considering what the investment assumption should be is to establish how
important the assumption is for the results of the modelling being performed. If, for example,
there is very little sensitivity to the investment assumption, then the actual value chosen for the
parameter would not be critical.
This point is also important when it comes to considering the size of the margins required for the
investment assumption.
The investment return will grow in significance as the likely benefit date is further into the
future (eg pre-funded long-term care insurance) or where guarantees are given (eg on
accelerated critical illness cover).
So, the main factors that lead to sensitivity to the investment assumption are the size of the
reserves built up, the duration of the policy and the investment guarantees given:
         The larger the reserves (relative to cashflows), the greater the proportion of total
cashflow (and profit) that arises from investment income, and hence the greater will be
the sensitivity to changes in the investment return.
         The longer the duration of the policy, the longer the reserves will earn investment
returns, and so the more significant they will be.
         The more onerous the guarantee(s), the more cautious the insurance company should be
in its asset selection. This caution should then be reflected in the investment return
assumption (because the expected return will differ according to the assets selected).
The extent of the guarantee is also very important for determining the size of the margins
that would be included in the investment return assumption.
Note that it is possible for the choice of investment assumption to be critical, and yet the contract
may be subject to very little investment risk (provided the correct investment assumption has
been made). The following question includes an example of this.
(1) Regular premium without\u2010p: Not very important: reserves are relatively small so that profits will be insensitive to changes in
Limited exposure, as investment income is of limited importance anyway.
(2) Regular premium without\u2010p: Important: the reserves are large and so a change in the assumption should make a significant
High, as cannot match with most of the liabilities until late in the term.
(3) Single premium without\u2010pr: Very important: the reserves are much larger for much longer than the regular premium case,
making investment return one of the key cashflow items of this contract.
The need for margins may not be as great as for (1), because it might be possible to invest in
matching assets – at least to the extent that the uncertainty of the claims costs allow – and
therefore secure a known investment yield when the policy is taken out.
Nevertheless, it remains of vital importance to get the investment assumption correct, ie equal to
(or lower than) the investment yield that the single premium will secure on the day that it is
Much lower than (2), but still some exposure to reinvestment risk due to uncertainty of when the
claims will start, and to the need to reinvest future investment income.
## (4) PMI: Policies are generally of one‐year duration, and claims are settled either during the year or
(usually) quite soon afterwards, so the investment return will not be important.
The process for determining the likely f:          consider the likely mix of assets that will back the contract in the future
         investigate the returns that such assets are yielding now (and in the past)
         attempt to predict the returns that will be obtained from the future asset mix bearing in
mind the impact of future changes to the economic environment.
In a ‘traditional’ pricing basis, the assumed rate of future earned investment return will
depend on the assets in which the reserves are invested, which in the case of health
insurance products tends to be fixed interest assets / bonds.
This is true where contracts provide benefits that are fixed in monetary terms. Where benefits
are inflation linked, index‐linked bonds would be preferred – provided they have good security
and are readily available.
Some equity‐based investment is also a possibility, in order to match real increases in benefit (or
expense) outgo.
LTCI, for example, would have a long expected investment period, and the need to mirror
increases in care costs (as far as possible) might prompt a more equity-based approach.
In general, though, asset types other than fixed interest or index‐linked bonds would only be
appropriate to match liabilities if benefits were neither fixed in monetary nor in real terms.
For example, some health insurance products operate in a unit-linked environment and some
assumption will be needed as to the returns available when profit testing these products.
Typically, a managed-fund approach is used, and the investment return would depend on
the expected mix of equities and bonds within the managed fund over the duration of the
policy.
This will give the unit‐fund growth rate assumption to use in the pricing model. In this case, a
separate assumption will also be needed for the investment return on the insurer’s non‐unit
reserves for these contracts.
While the assets that best match the contract’s liabilities are an important consideration, the
intended investment mix for the contract will not be derived from looking at the contract in
isolation. It will also be affected by the level of free assets available to the insurer to support
writing the business. This is because the extent of matching necessary to control the investment
risk will be less when there are more free assets, hence affecting the mix of assets used.
If a market-consistent approach is used (as was described in Subject CP1, see also Chapter
11 and Chapter 22), either deterministically or stochastically, then the expected investment
return can be set as the risk-free rate, irrespective of the actual underlying assets held (this
is the ‘risk neutral’ calibration approach).
However, if the stochastic approach is adopted then the investment return volatility and
correlation assumptions do remain dependent on the actual underlying asset type(s).
The extent of reinvestment risk: Reinvestment risk refers to the uncertainty of the return that can be obtained from investment in
the future. The best estimate of the return available from future investment may well differ from
the best estimate of the return available from investment now, and may also differ according to
how far into the future the investment is made. The overall best estimate investment assumption
will reflect the expected balance between the expected future and current investment yields.
If the actual cashflow is positive over a future period, then the insurer will need to buy assets.
The more that such future investment is expected to occur, the more the investment assumption
should reflect the expected future investment returns.
Even if the actual cashflow is predicted to be negative in the future, mismatching of assets and
liabilities by term may still mean that assets have to be sold and bought in the future, so that the
future reinvestment yields will still be important.
When pricing a new contract, all investment will be made in the future. Nevertheless, investment
returns will be far more certain at the point of sale compared to much later in the terms of those
contracts. The expected time between changes to the insurer’s pricing basis for a contract will
therefore also be important in establishing the best estimate investment assumption to use.
7 Taxes: The actuary will need to allow for the rates of tax applicable to the line of healthcare
insurance business in the territory where the business is being written.
The ways in which a State can levy taxes are numerous, but most will have impact on the
return that shareholders will get for supplying capital to finance the business and hence
must be considered in the premium basis.
7.1 Data and adjustments: When considering what future tax rates to assume, the best option is usually to use current rates,
allowing for any future changes that you know are going to happen.
Taxes on profits: The insurance company is taxed on the profit arising from the contracts being written (and
those already in force). In this case, none of the elements in the basis need to be altered,
but the return afforded by premiums over claims and expenses may need to be adjusted to
So the insurer needs to ensure that the expected present value of the future profits from each
policy, multiplied by [1  tax rate] , meets the target level of profit required. (See Chapter 19 for
In many countries, the taxes on profits are liable to reflect the level of reserves being
If the tax authorities permitted a strong reserving basis for calculating the profit used for tax
purposes, this would reduce the level of profit, and hence the amount of tax. (The tax is, in fact,
only delayed, but this will still reduce its value, possibly significantly.) So the tax authorities will
typically restrict the level of prudence permitted in the calculation of reserves in the tax
Taxes on investment income less expenses: Under this methodology, the insurer is taxed on the excess of investment income over the
expenses incurred. With this regime, the actuary includes investment income and expenses
net of the relevant tax rate. The return to the shareholders is thus the amended profit that
This is commonly known as an \u2018I \u2: In some territories where this has operated, the State noticed that heavy initial expenses
relieved many long-term insurers of paying tax in the foreseeable future; it thus changed the
rules to ‘allow’ expenses to offset the tax on investment income only on a staggered basis,
eg over the first three or seven years. The incorporation of this into a pricing calculation
requires profit testing techniques (which is likely to be the method used for all policy types
other than one‐year renewable contracts anyway).
The main thing is to make sure that the taxation structure is accurately included in the modelling.
Taxes on premiums: In some territories, insurance premiums are taxed by the State. Financially, the insurer
remains neutral to this levy, although the insurer may collect it on behalf of the State.
However, any additional charge on the premium makes the total cost of this line of
healthcare insurance more expensive and hence reduces its consumer attraction. The
actuary will often then look to other product designs that can produce contract formats that
are not liable to premium taxation.
So no direct allowance for taxation is needed in the pricing model when this method applies. The
insurer just needs to make sure that the premium that the customer is quoted and charged
includes the premium tax. For example, if premium tax is 6%, then the premium charged by the
insurer needs to include a 6% loading.
However, some assumptions in the pricing basis may be influenced by the level of tax on
premiums applying. For example, an increase in premium tax may reduce sales volumes and
increase lapse / non‐renewal rates. (This is another example of consistency.)
The chapter summary starts on the next p: keep all the chapter summaries together for revision purposes.
Benefit amount: Long‐term contracts usually have pre‐determined benefits. However, underlying claims
experience may vary with benefit size, therefore some rating adjustment may be made for
Average claim amounts have to be assessed for PMI. These should be based on own recent
experience and on market data, which may need to be adjusted for differences in:
\uf0b7 negotiated hospital contr: Alternatively, treatment price lists for private hospitals could be used.
Benefit inflation: For long‐term contracts, the sum insured may be inflated periodically. In this case, it is usual
to also inflate the premium. The rate of inflation may be an index of medical costs (if such
an index exists), consumer price inflation or a fixed rate.
Expenses: Expenses will be loaded for in accordance with the results of the most recent expense
investigation. If the insurer has insufficient recent experience, it might use industry or
Assumptions will be split by type of expense (initial, renewal, claim or termination), and
between per policy, per unit of premium or per unit of benefit.
For long‐term contracts, charging the same premium rates for all sizes of policy causes
cross‐subsidies between policies. This can be reduced or removed using individual
calculations, policy fees, or a sum insured differential.
Per‐policy contributions to fixed costs are made by reference to expected volumes. Care
must be taken to ensure competitiveness.
Expenses are higher than for life insurance due to a greater effort required to sell health and
care products and more work needed to underwrite and manage health claims.
Expense inflation: It is important to allow for the inflation of expenses, especially for long‐term products.
Expenses are typically linked to a combination of wage and price inflation.
Commission and clawback: Commission rates should be based on what will be paid, which will be influenced by current
levels of sales remuneration in the market as well as any legislation. Commission is generally
higher for health and care products than for life insurance due to the increased product
Special arrangements between salespeople and the insurer should be allowed for. A
weighted average may be needed if different rates are paid for the same product to different
Commission is usually expressed as a percentage of premium, but could alternatively be as a
percentage of the sum insured (or possibly as a fixed amount).
Clawback on early policy lapse should be allowed for, if appropriate. Allowances should be
made for the credit risk of the intermediary when setting clawback terms. Past experience
by distribution channel and product type will be the best source of information. Market and
reinsurers’ data may also be useful.
Investment return: The investment return assumption should be consistent with the inflation assumption(s).
\uf0b7 its significance for prof: –          the size of the reserves for the contract
–          the extent of any investment guarantees
         the intended asset mix for the contract – fixed interest or index‐linked securities are
         the extent of any reinvestment risk and the extent to which this can be reduced by a
The investment return assumption has little relevance for PMI, but is a big factor for LTCI.
Taxation: Tax assumptions will be based on known current rates plus any known future changes.
The practice questions start on the next: keep the chapter summaries together for revision purposes.

LTCI: Benefits under this policy are required to meet the costs of long‐term care, which mostly relate to
nursing and residential home costs. Linking the benefits to an index of such costs would seem to
be the most appropriate link for policyholders.
In many cases, and especially for LTCI, insurers put a ceiling on the rate of benefit inflation they
will pay, in order to contain their costs to be within affordable bounds.
Even though benefits and premiums may be increased at the same rate or in line with the same
index, an assumption will need to be made for this increase, as this will affect the final result. So,
whichever method is chosen for determining benefit increases, this should be explicitly included
Fixed percentage rates of increase require no assumption, but those linked to an external index
need an appropriate inflation rate assumption to be made. A similar approach to that used for
inflating expenses – see Section 0 below – would be followed.
2.2 Short\u2010term contracts (PMI): For products that aim to reimburse the policyholder, such as PMI and some types of LTCI
contracts, there is a need to make an assumption regarding the inflation of such costs – as
\uf0b7 any likely change in medi: A medical treatment protocol is the set of procedures adopted for a particular medical
treatment. A change in protocols may involve the use of new treatments, or require more
or less specialist medical staff to be used (consultants, anaesthetists, radiologists etc), any
of which may change the costs of treating a given acute condition.
\uf0b7 the future charging struc: A change in a hospital’s charging structure could involve, say, charging for the use of an
operating theatre according to the time taken to perform the operation, whereas in the
past it might have been a single lump sum charge for the type of operation, regardless of
how long the particular operation took.
\uf0b7 the future age profile of: Premium rates will normally vary by age, so it may be necessary to accommodate
age‐specific claim amount assumptions in the pricing model.
3 Expenses: The parameter values for expenses should reflect the expenses expected to be incurred in
processing and subsequently administering the business to be written under the product
3.1 Types of expenses: When thinking about expenses we can make a distinction between direct expenses and indirect
expenses (or overheads). The direct expenses are those that relate specifically to a class (or
classes) of business, whereas indirect expenses cannot be allocated directly to a class (or classes)
Note that the distinction between direct and indirect expenses is subjective – for example, the
property costs of the ‘new business administration’ department could be considered as part of
the direct initial administration expenses for CI business, or as part of the insurer’s overall
Furthermore, direct expenses can be either fixed or variable. Variable expenses are those that
depend in some way on the level of business – for example, they might vary by the number of
policies sold or by the amount of claims paid.
The expense assumptions should reflect the incidence of the direct expenses from each of the
         initial acquisition (eg initial commission and other marketing and sales costs)
\uf0b7 renewal administration:          renewal reward to sales channel (ie renewal commission, or similar)
Question: Which one of the main health and care insurance products (IP, CI, LTCI and PMI) will have larger
renewal costs than all the others, and why?
State, with reasons, which of the above types of expenses would not be important for PMI
contracts.
PMI will have the highest renewal admini: This is because, at renewal, the insurer has to send notices to each of its policyholders asking
them to agree to continue their insurance cover. This may or may not involve a change in policy
conditions at the time, and the circumstances of the policyholder may also have changed. It will
invariably involve a change in premium, which incurs the insurer in further costs (in the
assessment and calculation of the risk).
While the expenses of renewing a PMI policy will not be as high as that involved in insuring a PMI
policyholder for the first time, the costs will still be higher than for a long‐term contract each year.
In this latter case, all the insurer usually has to do is to inflate the premium and benefits as
required for its records, and receive and bank the premium.
Expense types that are not important for: Investment – The short‐term nature of PMI means that reserves are small, so there is little money
to invest. The investment expense assumption – along with the investment assumption itself –
will make little difference to the price of the contract, and would normally be ignored.
Withdrawal and termination – These are the same, as far as PMI is concerned, and are generally
referred to as lapses. They are simply policies that fail to renew at the renewal date, and the
expense of administering them tends to be swamped by the costs of renewal overall. Most of the
cost of non‐renewal is felt by having to spread all of the year’s renewal costs over the smaller
number of remaining policies that do renew.
There may also be some policies that cancel midway through the policy term (eg due to death or a
change in circumstances). These will incur costs, the level of which will be relatively minor.
In addition to covering the direct expenses, the expense assumptions should produce premiums
(or charges) that will make an appropriate contribution to the insurer’s indirect (overhead)
Note that per‐policy expenses are expenses that do not vary by policy size, for example policy
3.2 Data: The values (for the expense assumptions) will be determined after analysing the company’s
recent experience for the type of product concerned.
If the company has insufficient recent experience to provide meaningful results, or suitable
recent experience is not available, the parameter values may be based on a similar line, or
on any industry data or data from a reinsurance company.
3.3 Grouping the data: The result of this analysis will be a division of the expenses by function, and possibly by
whether the level of expense is expected to be proportional to the level of premium or
benefit. Alternatively, the result can be expressed as an amount per contract.
The latter division will be for variable expenses. In addition, it will also be useful to also identify
the level of fixed expenses associated with the product, and a suitable contribution to overheads.
More detail on the different types of expense is given in Chapter 29, where the mechanics of an
3.4 Loading expenses into premiums: Ideally, each policy should pay its share of expenses, including a contribution to overheads.
Unfortunately, this will not always be possible for practical reasons, and there will often be a
degree of cross‐subsidy between policies. Ensuring a competitive set of premiums might also be a
Expense loadings are often used as the optional margin to secure competitiveness; this can
only be done with careful monitoring and on a very short-term basis. Failure to cover
expenses is an important contributor to insolvency, as is failure to cover claims.
In the very price‐sensitive distribution channel of brokers, insurance companies have sometimes
set premiums for certain products with expense loadings that are only slightly greater than those
required to cover the variable expenses, thereby making an apparently insufficient contribution to
fixed expenses. This is then justified on the grounds that there is still some contribution to fixed
expenses, as opposed to the zero contribution that would result from trying (and failing) to sell an
uncompetitive product with a ‘correct’ contribution built in.
What really matters to the company is the total contribution to fixed expenses (and profit) that
results from selling the product as a whole, not per single policy.
Let’s now consider how certain types of expenses might be loaded into premium rates.
Expenses that vary with policy size: Expenses that vary with the premium size are easily loaded into prices as a percentage of
premium. An example of this is commission (which is discussed further in Section 5).
Per\u2010policy expenses: Recall that per‐policy expenses are expenses that do not vary with policy size and that the
premium rate for a policy may be expressed as £3 per £100 pa benefit. To allow properly for the
per‐policy expenses, it is therefore necessary to calculate a premium for a policy of average
benefit size, and then use the same rate of premium (per unit of benefit) for policies of any size.
This means that, while you might have thought of the expense loading as being per policy, it will
vary in practice in proportion to the size of the policy actually taken out.
Provided the assumption for the average policy size is borne out in practice, the insurer should
receive the per‐policy expense loadings that it assumed in its pricing basis. (Note that the average
benefit size is part of the business mix assumption, which is discussed in the next chapter.)
